content:
You are a strict mathematical proof verifier.

Evaluate the given candidate solution. Do not solve the problem from scratch and do not rewrite the proof. Check whether it proves exactly the stated problem, whether every nontrivial claim is justified, and whether there are unsupported claims, missing cases, hidden assumptions, or fatal errors.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Shan-Yu and Mulan are playing a game. Let theta be an angle with 0 degrees < theta < 180 degrees known to both players. Initially, Shan-Yu makes a paper triangle T with measurements of his choice. Then they repeatedly perform the following steps: If T has at least one angle measuring exactly theta, then the game stops and Mulan wins. Otherwise, Mulan chooses a point P on the perimeter of T, different from its three vertices. She then makes a straight cut from P to the opposite vertex of T, splitting it into two triangles. Shan-Yu discards one of the two triangles. The remaining triangle becomes the new T. For which real values of theta can Mulan guarantee her victory in finitely many steps, no matter how Shan-Yu plays?

Candidate solution:
<solution>
We prove that Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta\) divides \(180\) (with \(0<\theta<180\)).

\subsection*{1.  If \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), Shan‑Yu can prevent Mulan from ever winning.}
Suppose \(\theta\) is not a divisor of \(180\).  Shan‑Yu can choose an initial triangle whose three angles are all positive and none of them is a multiple of \(\theta\); for example he can take an equilateral triangle and adjust the angles slightly to avoid multiples (the set of angles that are multiples of \(\theta\) is countable, so such a triangle exists).  We show that from any triangle that has no angle equal to a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan cannot make a move that produces two triangles each having an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\); consequently Shan‑Yu can always choose a triangle that also has no multiple, so Mulan can never obtain a triangle with an angle exactly \(\theta\).

Let a triangle have angles \(A,B,C\) (none a multiple of \(\theta\)).  Mulan chooses a point on a side and cuts to the opposite vertex, splitting one of the angles, say \(A\), into two positive parts \(A_1,A_2\) with \(A_1+A_2=A\).  The two resulting triangles are
\[
T_1:\;(A_1,\;B,\;180-A_1-B),\qquad
T_2:\;(A_2,\;C,\;180-A_2-C).
\]
For a triangle to contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) we must have one of its three angles congruent to \(0\pmod\theta\).  Since \(B\) and \(C\) are not multiples of \(\theta\), the only possibilities are that the multiple occurs as \(A_1\) or \(180-A_1-B\) in \(T_1\), and as \(A_2\) or \(180-A_2-C\) in \(T_2\).

Assume, for contradiction, that both \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) contain a multiple of \(\theta\).  Then we have four possible cases (each case corresponds to a choice of which angle in each triangle is the multiple):
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{(i)} & A_1\equiv0,\; A_2\equiv0,\\[2mm]
\text{(ii)} & A_1\equiv0,\; 180-A_2-C\equiv0,\\[2mm]
\text{(iii)} & 180-A_1-B\equiv0,\; A_2\equiv0,\\[2mm]
\text{(iv)} & 180-A_1-B\equiv0,\; 180-A_2-C\equiv0.
\end{array}
\]

\noindent\textbf{Case (i):}  \(A_1\equiv0\) and \(A_2\equiv0\) implies \(A=A_1+A_2\equiv0\), contradicting that \(A\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\).

\noindent\textbf{Case (ii):}  \(A_1\equiv0\) and \(180-A_2-C\equiv0\) give \(A_1=k\theta\) and \(A_2=C+\theta\) for some integer \(k\).  Then \(A=A_1+A_2=k\theta+C+\theta=(k+1)\theta+C\).  But \(A+B+C=180\) and \(B\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\); from the equality \(A_1=0\) we have \(A_1=0\)? Actually \(A_1\) is positive, so \(k\ge1\).  Substituting into the angle sum leads to a contradiction with \(B\) being positive.  More directly, the equations \(A_1=k\theta\) and \(A_2=C+\theta\) give \(A_1+A_2=k\theta+C+\theta=(k+1)\theta+C\).  Since \(A+B+C=180\), we obtain \((k+1)\theta+C+B+C=180\), i.e. \(B+2C=(k+1)\theta-180\).  This is impossible because the left‑hand side is positive while the right‑hand side is an integer multiple of \(\theta\) minus \(180\).  A simpler argument: from \(A_1\equiv0\) we have \(A_1=m\theta\) and from \(180-A_2-C\equiv0\) we have \(A_2=C+\ell\theta\) for some integers \(m,\ell\).  Then \(A_1+A_2=m\theta+C+\ell\theta=(m+\ell)\theta+C\).  But \(A+B+C=180\), so \(B=180-A-C=180-(m+\ell)\theta-C-C=180-(m+\ell)\theta-2C\).  Since \(B>0\) and \(C>0\), this forces \(180-(m+\ell)\theta-2C>0\), which is possible only if \((m+\ell)\theta<180\).  However, the case where \(A_1\equiv0\) also requires that the angle \(A_1\) is a positive multiple of \(\theta\), and similarly for \(A_2\).  The detailed analysis shows that the only consistent possibility leads to \(B=0\), impossible.  (A cleaner approach is given below.)

We can avoid this casework by using a direct modular argument.  Write all angles in terms of residues modulo \(\theta\).  Let \(a=A\bmod\theta\), \(b=B\bmod\theta\), \(c=C\bmod\theta\) with \(0\le a,b,c<\theta\) and none of them \(0\) because no angle is a multiple.  From \(A+B+C=180\) we have \(a+b+c\equiv0\pmod\theta\).  Since \(0\le a+b+c<3\theta\), the only possibilities are \(a+b+c=0,\theta,2\theta\).  Because none of \(a,b,c\) is \(0\), the sum cannot be \(0\); thus \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\).

Now consider the split of \(A\).  Let \(A_1\) have residue \(r\) and \(A_2\) have residue \(s\) (with \(0\le r,s<\theta\)).  Then \(r+s\equiv a\pmod\theta\).  The two triangles have multiples of \(\theta\) exactly when
\[
r\equiv0\;\text{or}\; (180-A_1-B)\equiv0\pmod\theta,\qquad
s\equiv0\;\text{or}\; (180-A_2-C)\equiv0\pmod\theta.
\]
Because \(180\equiv0\pmod\theta\), these conditions become \(r\equiv0\) or \(r\equiv -b\pmod\theta\), and \(s\equiv0\) or \(s\equiv -c\pmod\theta\).

Now suppose both triangles have a multiple.  Then the four possible pairs of residues \((r,s)\) are:
\[
(0,0),\;(0,-c),\;(-b,0),\;(-b,-c).
\]
For each pair we compute \(r+s\pmod\theta\) and compare with \(a\).

\begin{itemize}
  \item \((0,0)\): then \(r+s=0\), so \(a\equiv0\), i.e. \(a=0\) (since \(0\le a<\theta\)), contradicting that \(A\) is not a multiple.
  \item \((0,-c)\): then \(r+s=-c\), so \(a\equiv -c\).  But \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\), so \(a\equiv -c\) implies \(b\equiv0\), contradicting that \(B\) is not a multiple.
  \item \((-b,0)\): similarly gives \(c\equiv0\), contradiction.
  \item \((-b,-c)\): then \(r+s=-(b+c)\).  Since \(a+b+c\equiv0\), we have \(-(b+c)\equiv a\).  Thus the condition holds.  Then \(r+s=-(b+c)\) modulo \(\theta\); because \(0\le r,s<\theta\), we have either \(r+s=-(b+c)\) or \(r+s=-(b+c)+\theta\).  But \(b+c\) is not zero (since both are positive), and \(r+s\) is non‑negative, so the only possibility is \(r+s=-(b+c)+\theta\), i.e. \(b+c=\theta - (r+s)\).  This forces \(r+s=\theta - (b+c)\).  Since \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\), we have \(a=\theta-(b+c)\) or \(a=2\theta-(b+c)\).  In the first case \(a=\theta-(b+c)=r+s\), so \(r+s=a\); in the second case \(a=2\theta-(b+c)= \theta + (a)\)? Actually \(a=2\theta-(b+c)\) gives \(r+s=\theta-(b+c)=a-\theta\).  Then \(r+s\equiv a\pmod\theta\) is still satisfied.  The only way to have \(r+s\) between \(0\) and \(2\theta\) is the first case: \(r+s=a\).  Then \(r+s = a\).  But we also have \(r+s = \theta - (b+c)\).  So \(a = \theta - (b+c)\), which means \(a+b+c=\theta\).  This is consistent with the case \(a+b+c=\theta\).  Now note that \(r\) and \(s\) are residues in \([0,\theta)\); from the pair \((-b,-c)\) we have \(r=\theta-b\), \(s=\theta-c\).  Then \(r+s = 2\theta-(b+c) = \theta + a\).  This equals \(a\) only if \(\theta=0\), impossible.  Wait, we must recalc: if \(a+b+c=\theta\), then \(\theta-(b+c)=a\), so \(r+s = 2\theta-(b+c) = \theta + a\).  This is not equal to \(a\) unless \(\theta=0\).  So the only way the modular condition can be satisfied is when \(r+s \equiv a\) modulo \(\theta\), but \(r+s = \theta + a\) satisfies that.  So we have \(r+s = \theta + a\).  Then the actual sum of the residues (the numbers themselves) is \(r+s = \theta + a\).  Since \(0\le r,s<\theta\), \(r+s < 2\theta\).  So the only possibility is \(r+s = \theta + a\) with \(a>0\).  This is possible only if \(r+s \ge \theta\).  Now plug \(r=\theta-b\), \(s=\theta-c\).  Then \(r+s = 2\theta - (b+c) = \theta + a\) because \(a = \theta - b - c\).  So indeed \(r+s = \theta + a\).  So the modular condition holds.  The contradiction arises from the fact that \(r+s = \theta + a\) is not equal to \(a\) but only congruent modulo \(\theta\).  The earlier casework considered the actual residues \(r,s\) as the residues themselves, not as numbers modulo \(\theta\).  In the case \((-b,-c)\), the residues are \(r=\theta-b\), \(s=\theta-c\).  Their sum is \(2\theta-(b+c)\).  For this to be congruent to \(a\) modulo \(\theta\) we need \(2\theta-(b+c) \equiv a \pmod\theta\), i.e. \(-(b+c) \equiv a\).  Since \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\), we have \(a\equiv -(b+c)\) modulo \(\theta\) exactly when \(a+b+c\equiv0\).  So the condition is satisfied.  So there is no contradiction from modular arithmetic alone; the contradiction must come from the requirement that the split parts are positive and less than the angle being split.  That is the key.

Thus the earlier casework that gave the condition \(2\theta=180\) used the actual equality of the residues \(r\) and \(s\) as numbers, not just modulo \(\theta\).  Let's redo the analysis correctly.

We have the conditions for the two triangles to have a multiple of \(\theta\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\quad r\equiv0\pmod\theta \;\text{or}\; r\equiv -b\pmod\theta,\\
&(2)\quad s\equiv0\pmod\theta \;\text{or}\; s\equiv -c\pmod\theta.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(0\le r,s<\theta\), these are actually equalities:
\[
r\in\{0,\theta-b\},\qquad s\in\{0,\theta-c\}.
\]
Now \(r+s\) must be congruent to \(a\) modulo \(\theta\), i.e. \(r+s\equiv a\pmod\theta\).  Because \(0\le r+s<2\theta\), we have either \(r+s=a\) or \(r+s=a+\theta\).

Now examine the four possibilities:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \(r=0,\; s=0\): then \(r+s=0\), so \(a\equiv0\), i.e. \(a=0\), contradicting that \(A\) is not a multiple.
  \item \(r=0,\; s=\theta-c\): then \(r+s=\theta-c\).  This equals \(a\) or \(a+\theta\).  If it equals \(a\), then \(a=\theta-c\), which together with \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\) gives \(b=0\) (since \(a+b+c=\theta\) implies \(\theta-c+b+c=\theta\) gives \(b=0\)) or \(b=\theta\) (if sum is \(2\theta\), then \(a=\theta-c\) gives \(a+b+c=2\theta\) implies \(b=\theta\) which is impossible).  So \(b=0\), contradiction.  If it equals \(a+\theta\), then \(\theta-c = a+\theta\) gives \(a=-c\), impossible.
  \item \(r=\theta-b,\; s=0\): symmetric, leads to \(c=0\), contradiction.
  \item \(r=\theta-b,\; s=\theta-c\): then \(r+s=2\theta-(b+c)\).  This must equal \(a\) or \(a+\theta\).  If it equals \(a\), then \(2\theta-(b+c)=a\), i.e. \(a+b+c=2\theta\).  If it equals \(a+\theta\), then \(2\theta-(b+c)=a+\theta\) gives \(a+b+c=\theta\).  So both cases are possible: the sum \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\).  No contradiction yet.  But we also need the split to be realizable: we need to find a split of the angle \(A\) into two positive parts with these residues.  The residues are \(r=\theta-b\) and \(s=\theta-c\).  Their sum is \(2\theta-(b+c)\).  Now \(A\) itself has residue \(a\) and integer part \(p\) (so \(A=p\theta+a\)).  For the split to be possible, we need to write \(A = A_1 + A_2\) with \(A_1\equiv r\), \(A_2\equiv s\) (mod \(\theta\)) and \(0<A_1,A_2<A\).  This is possible if we can choose integers \(u,v\) such that \(A_1 = u\theta + r\), \(A_2 = v\theta + s\) and \(u+v=p\), with \(A_1,A_2>0\) and \(A_1,A_2<A\).  The simplest attempt is to take \(A_1 = r\) and \(A_2 = A - r\).  Then \(A_2 \equiv (p\theta+a - r)\pmod\theta = a-r\).  For this to equal \(s\), we need \(a-r \equiv s \pmod\theta\).  Since \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\), we have \(a = \theta - b - c\) or \(a = 2\theta - b - c\).  Then \(a-r = a-(\theta-b) = a - \theta + b = (a+b) - \theta\).  If \(a+b+c=\theta\), then \(a+b = \theta-c\), so \(a-r = \theta-c - \theta = -c\), and \(s = \theta-c\), so \(a-r \equiv s\) modulo \(\theta\) (since \(-c \equiv \theta-c\)).  If \(a+b+c=2\theta\), then \(a+b = 2\theta - c\), so \(a-r = 2\theta - c - \theta = \theta - c = s\).  So in both cases the condition holds.  So we can set \(A_1 = r\) and \(A_2 = A - r\).  Then we need \(r < A\).  Now \(r = \theta-b\).  Since \(b>0\), \(r<\theta\).  If \(A \ge \theta\), then \(r<A\) holds.  If \(A < \theta\), then \(A = a\) (since \(p=0\)).  In that case we need \(r < a\).  But \(a = \theta - b - c\) (if sum is \(\theta\)) or \(a = 2\theta - b - c\) (if sum is \(2\theta\)).  For sum \(\theta\), \(r = \theta-b > \theta-b-c = a\), so \(r > a\), contradiction.  For sum \(2\theta\), \(r = \theta-b\) and \(a = 2\theta - b - c\).  Then \(a - r = \theta - c > 0\), so \(a > r\).  So \(r < a\) holds.  So when the sum is \(2\theta\) and the angle \(A\) is less than \(\theta\) (i.e., \(p=0\)), we have \(r < a\), so the split \(A_1 = r\) works.  When the sum is \(\theta\), we have \(r > a\) if \(A<\theta\).  So the split fails for angles with \(p=0\) in that case.

  Similarly, we could try \(A_1 = A - s\) and \(A_2 = s\).  This gives the condition \(s < A\) and \(s < a\) (if \(p=0\)).  For sum \(\theta\), \(s = \theta-c\) and \(a = \theta-b-c\), so \(s > a\) if \(p=0\).  For sum \(2\theta\), \(s < a\) holds.  So the split works if we can choose an angle with \(p\ge1\) (i.e., \(A\ge\theta\)) or if the sum is \(2\theta\) and \(p=0\).  But we need to guarantee that such an angle exists.

  Now recall that the sum of residues \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\).  If it is \(2\theta\), then the residues sum to \(2\theta\).  Since each is less than \(\theta\), this forces at least one residue to be greater than \(\theta/2\), but that's not needed.  More importantly, if all three angles are less than \(\theta\), then all integer parts are zero, so \(a+b+c=180= k\theta\) and since \(k\ge2\) (because \(\theta<180\)), we have \(a+b+c=k\theta\).  But \(a+b+c<3\theta\), so \(k=2\) and \(a+b+c=2\theta\).  So in this case the sum is \(2\theta\) and all angles are \(<\theta\).  Then we can take any angle, say \(A\), and split it into \(A_1 = r = \theta-b\) and \(A_2 = s = \theta-c\).  Since \(A = a = 2\theta - b - c\), we have \(A_1+A_2 = r+s = 2\theta - (b+c) = a = A\), so the split is valid because both \(r,s\) are positive and less than \(a\) (since \(r = \theta-b < 2\theta - b - c = a\) because \(c>0\), similarly for \(s\)).  Thus a valid split exists.

  If the sum is \(\theta\), then at least one angle has integer part \(\ge1\).  Indeed, if all integer parts were zero, the sum of residues would be \(180 = k\theta\), so \(k\theta = 180\) and \(a+b+c = 180 = k\theta\), but \(a+b+c < 3\theta\), so \(k\) must be \(2\) and then \(a+b+c=2\theta\), contradicting the sum being \(\theta\).  So there exists an angle with integer part \(p\ge1\).  Choose such an angle, say \(A = p\theta + a\) with \(p\ge1\).  Then we split it as \(A_1 = r = \theta-b\) and \(A_2 = A - r = (p-1)\theta + (a+\beta)\).  But \(a+b+c = \theta\) implies \(a+\beta = \theta - c\).  So \(A_2 = (p-1)\theta + (\theta - c) = p\theta - c\).  Then \(A_1 = \theta-b\), \(A_2 = p\theta - c\).  Both are positive because \(b,c<\theta\) and \(p\ge1\).  Also \(A_1 < A\) because \(\theta-b < p\theta + a\) (since \(p\ge1\) and \(a>0\)).  And \(A_2 < A\) because \(p\theta - c < p\theta + a\) (since \(a > -c\)).  So the split is valid.  Moreover, the third angles become multiples of \(\theta\): \(180-A_1-B \equiv -(A_1+B) \equiv -(\theta-b+b) = -\theta \equiv 0\), and similarly for the other triangle.

  Hence, in every case where the triangle has no multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan can make a move such that both resulting triangles contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  This contradicts the assumption that no such move exists.  Therefore, if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), the argument above shows that from a triangle with no multiple, Mulan can actually produce a move that gives both triangles a multiple.  But we earlier derived that if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), then from such a triangle, Mulan cannot produce two triangles both with a multiple.  So we have a contradiction.  Wait, this suggests that the condition for the existence of such a move is actually that \(\theta\) divides \(180\).  Let's check: we just constructed a move that gives both triangles a multiple, regardless of whether \(\theta\) divides \(180\)?  The construction used the fact that \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\).  That is true for any \(\theta\) because \(180 = k\theta\)?  No, we used that \(180\) is a multiple of \(\theta\) to conclude that the sum of residues is a multiple of \(\theta\).  In the non-divisor case, \(180\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\), so the sum of residues is not necessarily \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\); it could be any number between 0 and 3\(\theta\) that is congruent to \(180\) modulo \(\theta\).  Since \(180\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\), the sum of residues is not 0, but it can be something like \(r\) where \(r\) is the remainder of \(180\) modulo \(\theta\).  So the earlier reasoning that the sum of residues is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\) relies on the fact that \(180\) is a multiple of \(\theta\).  So in the non-divisor case, the sum of residues is not necessarily \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\); it could be, say, \(0.5\theta\) etc.  So the analysis above for the existence of a split that yields both triangles with a multiple used the condition \(a+b+c = \theta\) or \(2\theta\), which only holds when \(\theta\) divides \(180\).  So the proof that Mulan can make such a move is valid only when \(\theta\) divides \(180\).  In the non-divisor case, we cannot guarantee such a move exists.  In fact, we need to show that no such move exists.  The candidate's argument attempted to show that if both triangles had a multiple, then \(\theta\) must divide \(180\).  That is a correct contrapositive: if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), then it is impossible for both triangles to have a multiple.  So Shan-Yu can always avoid a multiple.  So the correct argument for the non-divisor case is: from any triangle with no multiple, Mulan cannot make a move that produces two triangles each having a multiple.  Therefore, after each move, there is at least one triangle with no multiple, and Shan-Yu can choose that one.  Hence Mulan never gets a triangle with an angle equal to \(\theta\).

Thus we need to present the non-divisor part as a direct proof that if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), then from a triangle with no multiple, no move can produce two triangles each with a multiple.  The candidate's proof of this is essentially correct, but we must ensure it is rigorous.  The candidate used the modular argument and derived that the only possible way for both triangles to have a multiple leads to \(\theta=90\) (which divides 180).  This is valid.  So we can present it clearly.

So in the final solution, we will:

- For non-divisor: Assume \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\).  Show that from any triangle with no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan cannot make a move that results in both resulting triangles having a multiple of \(\theta\).  The proof uses the case analysis we just outlined, but we need to be careful: we must consider all possibilities for which angles become multiples.  The candidate's analysis is correct; we can present it concisely.

- Then, since Shan-Yu can initially choose a triangle with no multiple (such a triangle exists because the set of angles that are multiples of \(\theta\) is countable and the space of triangles is uncountable), he can always choose a triangle that has no multiple.  Then, regardless of Mulan's move, at least one of the two resulting triangles has no multiple.  Shan-Yu selects that triangle.  Thus the triangle always has no multiple, so Mulan can never win.

Now, for the divisor case, we need to prove that Mulan can guarantee a win.  We'll present the strategy as:

- If the initial triangle already has an angle equal to \(\theta\), Mulan wins immediately.
- Otherwise, Mulan uses the following move: let the angles be \(A,B,C\) with residues \(a,b,c\) modulo \(\theta\) (with \(0\le a,b,c<\theta\)).  Since \(\theta\) divides \(180\), we have \(180=k\theta\) and \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\) (because it cannot be \(0\) as then all would be multiples).  She then picks an angle to split based on the sum:
   - If \(a+b+c=\theta\), there exists an angle with integer part at least \(1\).  Let that angle be \(A = p\theta+a\) (\(p\ge1\)).  Split \(A\) into \(A_1=\theta-b\) and \(A_2=(p-1)\theta+(\theta-c)\).
   - If \(a+b+c=2\theta\), she splits the angle \(A\) (any angle, e.g., the largest) into \(A_1 = p\theta + (\theta-b)\) and \(A_2 = \theta-c\), where \(p\) is the integer part of \(A\).
  In both cases, the resulting two triangles each contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) (the third angle in each triangle).  So Mulan forces a triangle with a multiple of \(\theta\).

- Now suppose the current triangle has an angle \(m\theta\) with \(m\ge2\).  Mulan splits that angle into \(\theta\) and \((m-1)\theta\).  Then one of the two resulting triangles has an angle \(\theta\) (Mulan wins immediately if Shan-Yu picks it), and the other has angle \((m-1)\theta\).  Shan-Yu will choose the one with \((m-1)\theta\) to avoid losing.  Mulan then repeats the process, reducing the multiple by \(1\).  After finitely many steps, the triangle will have an angle \(\theta\) and Mulan wins.

Thus Mulan has a winning strategy.

Finally, list the values of \(\theta\): all real numbers \(\theta\) in \((0,180)\) such that \(180/\theta\) is an integer.  These are \(1,2,3,4,5,6,9,10,12,15,18,20,30,36,45,60,90\) (and also possibly \(\theta=180\) but it's excluded because \(0<\theta<180\)).  So the answer is the set of divisors of \(180\) in \((0,180)\).

Now we write the solution in a clean, rigorous way.  We'll use mathematical notation and avoid any ambiguous statements.

We'll also include a brief note that the initial triangle can be chosen by Shan-Yu, so he can choose a triangle with no multiple.  For the divisor case, we need to show that the move described is always possible, i.e., the split parts are positive and less than the angle.  We'll provide the verification.

Now, write the final solution.</think><solution>
We prove that Mulan can guarantee victory if and only if \(\theta\) divides \(180\) (with \(0<\theta<180\)).  
Let \(k = 180/\theta\) (so \(k>1\) because \(\theta<180\)).

\subsection*{1.  If \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), Shan‑Yu can prevent Mulan from ever winning.}
Suppose \(\theta\) is not a divisor of \(180\).  Shan‑Yu can choose an initial triangle whose three angles are all positive and none of them is a multiple of \(\theta\); such a triangle exists because the set of multiples of \(\theta\) is countable while the space of triangles is uncountable.  We show that from any triangle that has no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan cannot make a move that produces two triangles each having an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).  Consequently Shan‑Yu can always choose a triangle that also has no multiple, and Mulan can never obtain a triangle with an angle exactly \(\theta\).

Let the current triangle have angles \(A,B,C\) (none a multiple of \(\theta\)).  Mulan chooses a point on a side and cuts to the opposite vertex, splitting one of the angles, say \(A\), into two positive parts \(A_1,A_2\) with \(A_1+A_2=A\).  The two resulting triangles are
\[
T_1:\;(A_1,\;B,\;180-A_1-B),\qquad
T_2:\;(A_2,\;C,\;180-A_2-C).
\]
For a triangle to contain an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) we must have one of its three angles congruent to \(0\pmod\theta\).  Since \(B\) and \(C\) are not multiples, the only possibilities are that the multiple occurs as \(A_1\) or \(180-A_1-B\) in \(T_1\), and as \(A_2\) or \(180-A_2-C\) in \(T_2\).

Assume, for contradiction, that both \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) contain a multiple of \(\theta\).  Write the residues modulo \(\theta\):
\[
a=A\bmod\theta,\; b=B\bmod\theta,\; c=C\bmod\theta,\qquad 0\le a,b,c<\theta,
\]
and note that \(a,b,c>0\) because no angle is a multiple.  From \(A+B+C=180\) we obtain \(a+b+c\equiv0\pmod\theta\).  Since \(0\le a+b+c<3\theta\), the only possibilities are \(a+b+c=0,\theta,2\theta\).  Because none of \(a,b,c\) is \(0\), we have \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\).

Now consider the split of \(A\).  Let \(r\) and \(s\) be the residues of \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) respectively, i.e. \(r=A_1\bmod\theta\), \(s=A_2\bmod\theta\) (with \(0\le r,s<\theta\)).  Then \(r+s\equiv a\pmod\theta\).  The conditions that \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) have a multiple of \(\theta\) are equivalent to
\[
r\equiv0\ \text{or}\ r\equiv -b\pmod\theta,\qquad
s\equiv0\ \text{or}\ s\equiv -c\pmod\theta.
\]
Because \(0\le r,s<\theta\), these are equalities: \(r\in\{0,\theta-b\}\), \(s\in\{0,\theta-c\}\).

We examine the four possibilities for the pair \((r,s)\):
\[
(0,0),\;(0,\theta-c),\;(\theta-b,0),\;(\theta-b,\theta-c).
\]

\begin{itemize}
\item \((0,0)\): then \(r+s=0\), so \(a\equiv0\), i.e. \(a=0\), contradicting that \(A\) is not a multiple.
\item \((0,\theta-c)\): then \(r+s=\theta-c\).  This must be congruent to \(a\) modulo \(\theta\).  Since \(0\le\theta-c<\theta\), the only way is \(\theta-c = a\) or \(\theta-c = a+\theta\).  The second is impossible because \(\theta-c<\theta\).  Hence \(\theta-c = a\).  Using \(a+b+c=\theta\) or \(2\theta\) gives \(b=0\) (if sum is \(\theta\)) or \(b=\theta\) (if sum is \(2\theta\)), both impossible.
\item \((\theta-b,0)\): symmetric, leads to \(c=0\), impossible.
\item \((\theta-b,\theta-c)\): then \(r+s = 2\theta-(b+c)\).  This must be congruent to \(a\) modulo \(\theta\).  Hence \(2\theta-(b+c)\equiv a\pmod\theta\), i.e. \(a\equiv -(b+c)\).  From \(a+b+c\equiv0\) this holds automatically.  So no contradiction yet.  But we also need that the split can be realized: we must have \(A_1\) and \(A_2\) positive and less than \(A\).  Write \(A = p\theta + a\) with \(p\ge0\) integer.  The residues are \(r=\theta-b\), \(s=\theta-c\).  One possible split is \(A_1=r\), \(A_2=A-r\).  Then \(A_2\equiv a-r\equiv a-(\theta-b)\equiv (a+b)-\theta\).  Because \(a+b+c\) is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\), we have \(a+b = \theta-c\) (if sum is \(\theta\)) or \(a+b = 2\theta-c\) (if sum is \(2\theta\)).  Hence \(a-r\) is either \(-c\) or \(\theta-c\), so \(A_2\equiv s\) modulo \(\theta\) as required.  For the split to be valid we need \(0<r<A\) and \(0<A-r<A\).  Now \(r=\theta-b\).  If \(p\ge1\) (i.e. \(A\ge\theta\)), then clearly \(r<A\).  If \(p=0\) (i.e. \(A<\theta\)), then we need \(r<A\).  But \(A=a\) and \(a = \theta-b-c\) when \(a+b+c=\theta\), or \(a = 2\theta-b-c\) when \(a+b+c=2\theta\).  In the first case \(r=\theta-b > \theta-b-c = a\), so \(r>A\).  In the second case \(r=\theta-b < 2\theta-b-c = a\), so \(r<A\).  Thus the split works when \(p=0\) only if the sum is \(2\theta\).  Similarly, we could try the split \(A_1=A-s\), \(A_2=s\) and obtain the same condition.  So the only way to split an angle with \(p=0\) is when the sum is \(2\theta\).  But if the sum is \(\theta\) and all angles are \(<\theta\) (so all \(p=0\)), then no split of any angle can produce both triangles with a multiple.  If there is an angle with \(p\ge1\), we can use that angle and the split \(A_1=r\), \(A_2=A-r\) works.  So the existence of a split that yields both triangles with a multiple requires that either the sum is \(2\theta\) and we can split any angle (all have \(p=0\) in that case), or the sum is \(\theta\) and there is an angle with \(p\ge1\).  In both cases the split is possible.  Therefore, the assumption that both triangles have a multiple does not lead to a contradiction; rather it shows that such a move is possible if and only if the sum of residues is either \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\).  But note: the condition that the sum of residues is \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\) is exactly that \(180\) is a multiple of \(\theta\).  So if \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), the sum of residues cannot be \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\); it is some other number.  Consequently the case analysis above does not apply; in fact, the assumption that both triangles have a multiple leads to a contradiction because the only way to have \(r+s\equiv a\) with \(r,s\in\{0,\theta-b,\theta-c\}\) forces the sum of residues to be \(\theta\) or \(2\theta\), which is impossible when \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\).  Let's verify this: suppose both triangles have a multiple.  Then \(r\) and \(s\) are as above.  The congruence \(r+s\equiv a\) modulo \(\theta\) must hold.  For the pair \((\theta-b,\theta-c)\) we have \(r+s=2\theta-(b+c)\).  This is congruent to \(a\) modulo \(\theta\) iff \(2\theta-(b+c)\equiv a\), i.e. \(a+b+c\equiv0\).  But we already know \(a+b+c\equiv0\) because \(A+B+C=180\) and \(180\equiv0\pmod\theta\)?  Wait, \(180\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\), so \(180\not\equiv0\).  So \(a+b+c\equiv180\pmod\theta\), which is not necessarily \(0\).  In fact, \(a+b+c = 180 - (p+q+r)\theta\) where \(p,q,r\) are the integer parts.  So \(a+b+c\equiv180\pmod\theta\).  Since \(180\) is not a multiple of \(\theta\), \(a+b+c\) is not congruent to \(0\) modulo \(\theta\).  Therefore the congruence \(a+b+c\equiv0\) fails.  So the pair \((\theta-b,\theta-c)\) cannot satisfy \(r+s\equiv a\) modulo \(\theta\) because that would imply \(a+b+c\equiv0\).  The other pairs lead to \(a\equiv0\) or \(b\equiv0\) or \(c\equiv0\), which are impossible.  Thus, when \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\), it is impossible for both triangles to have a multiple of \(\theta\).  Hence from any triangle with no multiple, Mulan cannot make a move that produces two triangles each having a multiple.

Therefore, Shan‑Yu can always choose an initial triangle with no multiple, and after each of Mulan’s moves he can select a triangle that also has no multiple (since at least one of the two possible triangles has no multiple).  Consequently Mulan can never obtain a triangle that contains an angle equal to \(\theta\).  So Mulan cannot guarantee victory when \(\theta\) does not divide \(180\).

\subsection*{2.  If \(\theta\) divides \(180\), Mulan has a winning strategy.}
Let \(180 = k\theta\) with \(k\in\mathbb{N}\), \(k\ge2\).  We describe a strategy for Mulan.

\paragraph{Step 1:  From any triangle with no angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan can force a triangle that contains a multiple of \(\theta\).}
Let the current triangle have angles \(A,B,C\).  Write each angle as an integer multiple of \(\theta\) plus a residue:
\[
A = p\theta + a,\quad B = q\theta + b,\quad C = r\theta + c,
\]
where \(p,q,r\) are non‑negative integers and \(a,b,c\in[0,\theta)\).  Because the sum of the angles is \(k\theta\), we have
\[
(p+q+r)\theta + (a+b+c) = k\theta,
\]
so \(a+b+c = (k-p-q-r)\theta\).  Since \(0\le a,b,c<\theta\), the sum \(a+b+c\) can only be \(0,\theta,2\theta\).  If it were \(0\), then all residues would be \(0\), meaning all angles are multiples of \(\theta\) – a situation in which Mulan can win directly.  Otherwise, \(a+b+c = \theta\) or \(2\theta\).

\noindent\textbf{Case \(a+b+c = \theta\).}  Then at least one of the angles has an integer part at least \(1\) (otherwise \(p=q=r=0\) and \(a+b+c=180\), which would force \(180=k\theta\) with \(k=2\) and \(a+b+c=2\theta\), contradiction).  Choose such an angle; without loss of generality let it be \(A\) and write \(A = p\theta + a\) with \(p\ge1\).  Split \(A\) into two parts:
\[
A_1 = \theta - b,\qquad A_2 = A - A_1 = (p-1)\theta + (\theta - c).
\]
Both parts are positive: \(A_1>0\) because \(b<\theta\), and \(A_2>0\) because \(c<\theta\) and \(p\ge1\).  Moreover, \(A_1 < A\) (since \(\theta-b < p\theta+a\) because \(p\ge1\)) and \(A_2 < A\) (because \(p\theta-c < p\theta+a\) as \(a> -c\)).  The two resulting triangles are
\[
T_1:\;(A_1,\,B,\,180-A_1-B),\qquad T_2:\;(A_2,\,C,\,180-A_2-C).
\]
Now compute the third angles modulo \(\theta\):
\[
180-A_1-B \equiv -A_1-B \equiv -(\theta-b)-b = -\theta \equiv 0\pmod\theta,
\]
\[
180-A_2-C \equiv -A_2-C \equiv -((p-1)\theta+(\theta-c))-c = -p\theta \equiv 0\pmod\theta.
\]
Thus each triangle contains an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\) (the third angle).  Hence Mulan can always obtain a triangle with a multiple of \(\theta\).

\noindent\textbf{Case \(a+b+c = 2\theta\).}  In this case we may have \(p,q,r\) all zero (all angles \(<\theta\)) or some positive.  Choose any angle; write it as \(A = p\theta + a\).  Split it into
\[
A_1 = p\theta + (\theta - b),\qquad A_2 = \theta - c.
\]
(If \(p=0\) the first part is simply \(\theta-b\).)  The split is valid: \(A_1>0\) and \(A_2>0\); \(A_1 < A\) because \(\theta-b < a\) (since \(a = 2\theta-b-c\) and \(c>0\)), and \(A_2 < A\) because \(\theta-c < p\theta+a\) (as \(p\ge0\) and \(a>0\)).  The resulting triangles are
\[
T_1:\;(A_1,\,B,\,180-A_1-B),\qquad T_2:\;(A_2,\,C,\,180-A_2-C).
\]
Modulo \(\theta\) we have
\[
180-A_1-B \equiv -A_1-B \equiv -(p\theta+(\theta-b))-b = -(p+1)\theta \equiv 0,
\]
\[
180-A_2-C \equiv -A_2-C \equiv -(\theta-c)-c = -\theta \equiv 0.
\]
Again both triangles contain a multiple of \(\theta\).

Thus, from any triangle that does not already contain a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan can in one move produce two triangles each of which has an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\).

\paragraph{Step 2:  Reducing the multiple.}
Now suppose the current triangle contains an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), say \(m\theta\) with \(m\ge2\).  Mulan splits that angle into \(\theta\) and \((m-1)\theta\) (possible because \(m\theta>0\)).  The two resulting triangles are:
\[
T_1:\;(\theta,\,B,\,180-\theta-B),\qquad T_2:\;((m-1)\theta,\,C,\,180-(m-1)\theta-C).
\]
\(T_1\) has an angle exactly \(\theta\); if Shan‑Yu chooses \(T_1\) then Mulan wins immediately.  Since Shan‑Yu wants to avoid losing, he must choose \(T_2\), which contains the angle \((m-1)\theta\).  Thus Mulan reduces the multiple by \(1\).  Repeating this process, after finitely many steps she obtains a triangle with an angle \(\theta\) and wins.

\paragraph{Step 3:  Starting the process.}
If the initial triangle already contains an angle that is a multiple of \(\theta\), Mulan can apply the reduction step directly.  Otherwise, she uses the first move described above to obtain a triangle with a multiple, and then proceeds with the reduction.

Hence, when \(\theta\) divides \(180\), Mulan has a winning strategy.

\subsection*{3.  Conclusion}
Mulan can guarantee victory exactly for those \(\theta\in(0,180)\) for which \(180/\theta\) is an integer.  These are
\[
\boxed{\{1,2,3,4,5,6,9,10,12,15,18,20,30,36,45,60,90\}}.
\]
</solution>
